skill.name: ai-avatar-video
skill.description: Create AI avatar and talking head videos with inference.sh CLI workflows for portraits, speech, lipsync, and dubbing.
skill.summary: Generate talking avatar videos from portraits, scripts, audio, or existing video assets.
skill.author: inference-skills

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many shell backtick and URL patterns, but the backticks are Markdown code fences and examples, not hidden executable code. The skill is still medium risk because it authorizes belt CLI usage and sends user media, scripts, and URLs to remote inference services.

medium_findings.title: Remote Inference CLI Workflow
medium_findings.description: The skill permits Bash access for belt commands and provides many belt app run examples. This is legitimate for the skill purpose, but it can transmit user images, audio, video, and scripts to remote inference services.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines show explicit belt CLI authorization and remote app run workflows. The commands match the skill purpose, so the risk is data exposure through intended use rather than malicious execution.

medium_findings.title: Networked Media Inputs and Documentation Links
medium_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs are service links, documentation links, or placeholder media URLs used in examples. They are not credential exfiltration, but users should avoid private media unless the remote service is approved.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible in examples and documentation references, with no hidden request logic. Network use is central to the remote video-generation workflow, so publication should include a data-sharing warning.

low_findings.title: Static Backtick and Cryptography Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The Ruby shell backtick alerts correspond to Markdown code fences, inline code, model identifiers, and command examples. The weak cryptography alert at the description line has no supporting cryptographic code or algorithm evidence.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The only scanned file is Markdown, and the cited regions contain documentation examples. I did not find evidence of Ruby execution syntax or cryptographic operations in SKILL.md.

dangerous_patterns.title: Assistant-Callable External CLI
dangerous_patterns.description: The frontmatter allows Bash commands matching belt. This can trigger remote inference jobs, so user approval and input review are important before execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The allowed-tools entry is explicit and scoped to belt. The restriction lowers command-injection concern, but the tool still performs external network operations.

content.value_statement: Teams need fast avatar videos without building a production pipeline. This skill provides guided CLI workflows for portraits, speech, lipsync, and multilingual dubbing.
actual_capabilities: Runs inference.sh belt workflows for P-Video-Avatar, OmniHuman, Fabric, and PixVerse Lipsync.
Creates talking avatar videos from a portrait and text script using built-in TTS.
Uses existing speech audio to drive lipsync for portraits or videos.
Combines portrait generation, TTS, transcription, translation, and lipsync into content workflows.
Compares supported avatar models by speed, cost, output style, and TTS support.
limitations: Requires the inference.sh belt CLI and a valid account before generation can run.
Sends media URLs, text scripts, and audio inputs to remote inference services.
Does not verify likeness rights, consent, or brand approval for generated avatars.
Output quality depends on portrait quality, audio clarity, model limits, and service availability.

use_cases.title: Product Demo Presenter
use_cases.description: Create short avatar-led product walkthroughs from an approved portrait and a prepared script.

use_cases.title: Localized Training Clips
use_cases.description: Generate translated speech and lipsync existing presenter videos for internal learning materials.

use_cases.title: Character Dialogue Prototype
use_cases.description: Turn generated character portraits into quick talking-head scenes for story and game prototypes.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Simple Avatar Video
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this approved portrait URL and short script to create a 720p talking avatar video with P-Video-Avatar.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Avatar Models
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare P-Video-Avatar, OmniHuman, Fabric, and PixVerse for my use case, then recommend one model with reasons.

prompt_templates.title: Build a TTS and Lipsync Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Plan the steps to generate speech from this script, then use that audio to drive a talking-head video.

prompt_templates.title: Localize an Existing Video
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a workflow to transcribe this video, translate the script, generate new speech, and lipsync the original video.

output_examples.input: A user provides a portrait URL, a 30-second welcome script, and requests a clear English voice.
output_examples.output: The skill selects P-Video-Avatar, prepares the generation steps, and explains expected inputs, resolution choices, and privacy considerations.

output_examples.input: A training team wants a Spanish version of an existing presenter clip.
output_examples.output: The skill outlines transcription, translation review, speech generation, and final lipsync with checkpoints for quality and consent.

output_examples.input: A creator asks which avatar model is best for a multi-person image.
output_examples.output: The skill recommends OmniHuman for multi-character control and notes tradeoffs in speed, cost, and audio requirements.

best_practices: Use only portraits, voices, and scripts that you have permission to process and publish.
Review all generated commands before execution, especially URLs, scripts, voices, and output settings.
Start with short low-resolution tests before running long or high-resolution video jobs.
anti_patterns: Do not upload private, biometric, confidential, or regulated media without approval.
Do not create impersonation, endorsement, or identity-sensitive avatar content without clear consent.
Do not assume generated speech, translations, or lip movements are accurate without human review.

faq.question: Does this skill generate videos locally?
faq.answer: No. It uses the inference.sh belt CLI to run remote inference apps.

faq.question: Which model should most users try first?
faq.answer: The skill recommends P-Video-Avatar for speed, cost, built-in TTS, and general avatar workflows.

faq.question: Can it use my own audio file?
faq.answer: Yes. Several examples show using an audio URL instead of built-in text-to-speech.

faq.question: Can it dub an existing video?
faq.answer: Yes. It describes a workflow for transcription, translation, speech generation, and lipsync.

faq.question: What permissions are needed for safe use?
faq.answer: Use approved likenesses, voices, scripts, and brand assets. Get consent before generating or publishing identity-based media.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: The skill is not malicious, but it runs a networked CLI and sends user media to remote services.
